nuggets.headline: Even for a band as acclaimed as Radiohead, the making of *In Rainbows* was fraught with internal challenges, with members questioning if the band would even continue.
nuggets.text: Following their 2003 album *Hail to the Thief*, Radiohead entered a period of public struggles in the studio and blossoming side projects that led many to believe the band might be 'kaput.' Their eventual return with *In Rainbows* felt like a 'resurrection' to fans, especially as it coincided with their newfound freedom after their 12-year contract with EMI expired, making them 'unemployed' to create on their own terms.

source.title: Radiohead's 'In Rainbows' Turns 10: The Surprise Album's Lasting Legacy | Billboard
source.quoteSnippet: When a band you once feared dead returns out of nowhere with one of their greatest albums, it feels a little like a resurrection... the biggest surprise was that a new Radiohead album existed at all.

nuggets.imageCaption: Thom Yorke performing during V Festival in 2006, prior to *In Rainbows*' release.

nuggets.headline: The rhythmic bedrock of 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' might seem understated, but its constant, unchanging drum pattern is a deliberate, anti-pop choice that orchestrates the song's profound textural build.
nuggets.text: Unlike most pop arrangements where drums delineate verse-chorus sections, 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' maintains a solid, consistent drum groove throughout much of its runtime. This unconventional decision allows the song's intricate guitar arpeggios and other instrumental layers to gradually increase in density and complexity, creating a subtle, immersive momentum that eschews traditional structural signaling.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: The track starts with a solid drum groove, which will stay the same throughout most of the song. This choice is quite interesting since it implies that the drums will *not* signal the subdivision of the arrangement in sections, as is usually how drum arrangements function in pop music.

nuggets.headline: If the driving, distinct guitar patterns and undeniable alternative rock energy of 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' resonate with you, then it's time to rediscover the sharp, kinetic sound of The Strokes.
nuggets.text: While Radiohead explores atmospheric depths with intricate arpeggios, The Strokes bring a similar mastery of compelling and distinctive guitar interplay to a more immediate, garage-rock-inspired sound. Their tightly woven, often dual-guitar attack and cool, melodic sensibility offer a complementary, equally captivating take on guitar-driven indie rock.

source.title: Similar artists - Thom Yorke
source.quoteSnippet: The Strokes [32% match] are listed as a similar artist by listeners who enjoy Thom Yorke and Radiohead, showcasing a shared audience for influential alternative rock.

artistSummary: Radiohead stands as an influential alternative rock band, celebrated for their innovative sound and experimental evolution from guitar-driven rock to complex electro-conceptualists. Their album 'In Rainbows' marked a pivotal moment, showcasing not only artistic independence after leaving their label but also a groundbreaking 'pay-what-you-wish' release model that challenged industry norms.
